messages.content: The southwestern part of Australis is the only other place in the world with trees as large as those found in the Temperate Evergreen Rainforest of the western US. true or false

False. The southwestern part of Australia is not known for its large trees, and the only other place in the world with trees as large as those found in the Temperate Evergreen Rainforest of the western US is the temperate rainforests of New Zealand.

A swamp is an area with poorly-drained soils that creates a wetland ecosystem dominated by grasses.
Group of answer choices

True

False
False. A swamp is an area with poorly-drained soils that creates a wetland ecosystem dominated by trees and shrubs, while a wetland dominated by grasses is called a marsh.

Maple-Beech-Birch forests tend to be found in the drier and cooler regions of the eastern US because they are both cold tolerant AND drought tolerant.
Group of answer choices

True

False

False. Maple-Beech-Birch forests tend to be found in the wetter and cooler regions of the eastern US, as these trees prefer moist soils and are not particularly drought tolerant. While they are cold hardy, this is not the main reason for their distribution.
Deciduous angiosperms are better than gymnosperms at tolerating high light intensity. For example, almost no UV light penetrates the leaves of angiosperm plants.

False. Gymnosperms are better adapted to tolerate high light intensity, as their needles or scales help to reduce moisture loss and damage from intense light. In contrast, many angiosperms have broad leaves that are more vulnerable to damage from high light intensity and UV radiation. Some angiosperms have developed adaptations to reduce this damage, such as thick or waxy coatings on their leaves or the ability to adjust the orientation of their leaves to minimize exposure to intense sunlight.

Trees in the eastern US forests generally attain greater heights than in the western US due to the absence of storms that generate high wind speeds.
Group of answer choices

True

False

False. Trees in the western US can attain greater heights than in the eastern US, due to milder temperatures, cooler and more frequent fog, and longer growing seasons. The larger size of some western US trees, such as coast redwoods and giant sequoias, is also partly due to their ability to withstand strong winds, which can help them reach heights beyond what eastern US species can achieve.

Generally speaking, the forests in the pacific northwest have a greater amount of coarse woody debris than other forested ecosystems. This is mainly due to one factor - the large diameter of the tree stems that dominate this region.
Group of answer choices

True

False

True. The forests in the Pacific Northwest typically have a greater amount of coarse woody debris, which consists of fallen and decaying logs and branches, than other forested ecosystems. This is due in part to the large diameter of the tree stems that dominate this region, such as Douglas-fir and western red cedar, which produce more woody material as they grow and may live for centuries or more, allowing ample time for falling and decay. The moist and mild climate of the Pacific Northwest also promotes rapid decomposition of woody debris, which may contribute to the high amount of such material present in these forests.

Temperate Evergreen Rainforests in the pacific northwest have nearly twice the standing aboveground biomass as an eastern deciduous forest because they grow nearly twice as much each year.
Group of answer choices

True

False

False. Temperate Evergreen Rainforests in the Pacific Northwest do have much more standing aboveground biomass than Eastern deciduous forests, but this is not due to growing nearly twice as much each year. Instead, the productivity of the Pacific Northwest forests is high due to a combination of factors, including a longer growing season, abundant precipitation, and mild temperatures, as well as relatively infrequent natural disturbances such as wildfire. These factors allow for the accumulation of large amounts of biomass over time. In contrast, many Eastern deciduous forests experience more frequent disturbances such as windstorms, ice storms, and insect outbreaks, which may limit the amount of standing biomass.

In our discussion of the eastern US forests, we talked about how the exclusion of fire from ecosystems has allowed changes in the vegetation of many ecosystems over the last century. This vegetation change resulting from fire exclusion is occurring in more than one of the physiographic regions we discussed in the eastern US.
Group of answer choices

True

False

True. The exclusion of fire from ecosystems has allowed changes in vegetation to occur in several of the physiographic regions we discussed in the eastern US, including the Appalachian Mountains, the Piedmont region, and the Coastal Plain. In some cases, fire suppression has allowed fire-intolerant species to encroach upon historically fire-adapted ecosystems, altering the composition and structure of these communities. In other cases, fire suppression has led to a buildup of fuel and an increased risk of catastrophic wildfires.

Once buds have been produced on a deciduous angiosperm tree, they are able to survive all winter conditions and produce new leaves in the spring.
Group of answer choices

True

False

False. While some buds on deciduous angiosperm trees are able to survive harsh winter conditions, others may be damaged or killed by factors such as freezing temperatures, desiccation, or herbivory. The fate of buds may vary depending on their location on the tree, as well as on the species and its specific adaptations to winter conditions. Even buds that survive the winter may not necessarily produce new leaves in the spring, as this depends on a variety of factors such as the availability of water and nutrients, as well as suitable temperatures and daylengths for growth.

Four of the forest cover types that occur in the eastern US are listed below, from this list circle the forest cover type that is the most drought tolerant.
Group of answer choices

Oak-Hickory

Aspen-Birch

Maple-Beech-Birch

Elm-Ash-Cottonwood
Oak-Hickory.

In which of the following eastern US regions are people most concerned about restoring forests to help prevent agricultural run-off from impacting aquatic ecosystems?
Group of answer choices

Lower Mississippi Riverine

Piedmont Plateau

Oauchita

Coastal-Atlantic Plains
Coastal-Atlantic Plains.

In the lecture on eastern US forests we talked about which forest cover types best tolerate: cold temperatures/short growing season, fire, drought, and flooding.  Which two stressors have the most forest cover types in common?
Group of answer choices

fire & drought

fire & flooding

flooding & cold temperatures/short growing season

cold temperatures/short growing season & fire
The two stressors with the most forest cover types in common are fire and drought.

Select the physiographic region below that has the second most diverse temperate forest in the world?
Group of answer choices

Blueridge & Newer Appalachians

Adirondacks

Ozark Plateau & Ouachitas

Lower Mississippi Riverine Forest
Blueridge & Newer Appalachians.

In the lecture on eastern US forests we talked about which forest cover types best tolerate: cold temperatures/short growing season, fire, drought, and flooding. Which two stressors have the most forest cover types in common?
Group of answer choices

fire & drought

fire & flooding

flooding & cold temperatures/short growing season

cold temperatures/short growing season & fire                                Based on: Cold and/or short growing season
• Spruce-fir
• Red-White-Jack pine (dry sites)
• Aspen-Birch (wet sites)
• Maple-Beech-Birch
Flooding
• Oak-Gum-Cypress (warm)
• Elm-Ash-Cottonwood (cool)
• Aspen-Birch (cold)
• Longleaf-Slash (tolerates wetter sites than other pines)
Fire & Drought
• Pine Forests
• Oak-Pine
• Oak –Hickory
Note: Oaks better at tolerating seasonal drought
The two stressors that have the most forest cover types in common are fire and drought.

The southeastern part of the US receives a lot of precipitation, yet it is dominated by drought tolerant forest cover types.  Why are drought tolerant forests so successful in this area?
Group of answer choices

Although the area receives a lot of precipitation, the coarse soils allow the precipitation to drain from the soils quickly so there is little soil moisture remaining available for plant growth.

Although the area receives a lot of precipitation, it all comes during the winter months. And so during the summer, when plants are growing, there is little soil moisture remaining for plant growth.

Although the area receives a lot of precipitation there are very few nutrients in the soil. Since drought tolerant mechanisms are similar to nutrient limitation mechanisms, drought tolerant forests tend two grow well in these conditions.

The Southeastern US is dominated by Maple-Beech-Birch forests, which are not drought tolerant.

False. The southeastern part of the US is not dominated by Maple-Beech-Birch forests, which are found in the cooler and wetter regions of the eastern US. The most common forest cover types in the southeastern US are pine forests, which are generally better adapted to cope with less available moisture than some other forest types. Pine forests are able to capture and store water with their long taproots, and they often have tall, curvy trunks and needle-like leaves that reduce water loss through transpiration. In addition, pine forests can be resilient to occasional droughts and wildfires, which can be important factors in shaping forest ecosystems in this region.

The southeastern part of the US receives a lot of precipitation, yet it is dominated by drought tolerant forest cover types.  Why are drought tolerant forests so successful in this area?
Group of answer choices

A. Although the area receives a lot of precipitation, the coarse soils allow the precipitation to drain from the soils quickly so there is little soil moisture remaining available for plant growth.

B. Although the area receives a lot of precipitation, it all comes during the winter months. And so during the summer, when plants are growing, there is little soil moisture remaining for plant growth.

C. Although the area receives a lot of precipitation there are very few nutrients in the soil. Since drought tolerant mechanisms are similar to nutrient limitation mechanisms, drought tolerant forests tend two grow well in these conditions.

D. The Southeastern US is dominated by Maple-Beech-Birch forests, which are not drought tolerant.

False. The southeastern part of the US is not dominated by Maple-Beech-Birch forests, which are found in the cooler and wetter regions of the eastern US. The most common forest cover types in the southeastern US are pine forests, which are generally better adapted to cope with less available moisture than some other forest types. Pine forests are able to capture and store water with their long taproots, and they often have tall, curvy trunks and needle-like leaves that reduce water loss through transpiration. In addition, pine forests can be resilient to occasional droughts and wildfires, which can be important factors in shaping forest ecosystems in this region.
Which Letter is the answer?
The answer is D.
does the SW usa have coarse soil?

The soil texture in the southwestern USA can vary depending on the specific location, but in general, much of the region has coarse and sandy soils. This is partly due to the arid and semi-arid conditions in much of the region, which leads to a lack of organic matter and nutrient cycling in the soil. In addition, the soils in some areas may have been influenced by volcanic activity or erosion from nearby mountains, leading to the development of more coarse and rocky soils.
What about the SE?

In the southeastern USA, soil types can vary depending on the specific physiographic region and local conditions. In general, the soil in the southeastern USA is diverse and includes a range of soil textures, from sandy to clayey. The region has a humid subtropical climate, which can lead to higher levels of organic matter in the soil due to the growth of vegetation. However, soil characteristics can be influenced by a variety of factors, such as topography, geology, and land use history, so soil types can vary widely across the region.

Which physiographic region is known for having very little topographic relief and only has a change in elevation of ~300 feet across the entire region?
Group of answer choices

Gulf-Atlantic Coastal Plain & Piedmont Plateau

St. Lawrence Valley

Adirondacks

Appalachian Plateau & Interior Plains
Gulf-Atlantic Coastal Plain & Piedmont Plateau.

You can see from the figure below that the boreal forest stores a lot more carbon than either tropical or temperate forests.  What are the two main reasons for this? Select both correct answers.                   
The permafrost layer, which 'locks' away organic matter so it cannot be decomposed.

The large area of land that the boreal forest occurs on.

The large investment of biomass into roots

The high LMA values of the leaves

The short growing season in the regions boreal forests occur.

The two main reasons for the boreal forest storing more carbon than tropical or temperate forests are:
- The large area of land that the boreal forest occurs on.
- The short growing season in the regions boreal forests occur.

The Floral diversity in the boreal forest results from the large diversity of:
Group of answer choices

Moss/lichens

Insects

Willow Species

Tree Species

Grass Species

Alpine Species

The floral diversity in the boreal forest results from the large diversity of tree species, which include conifers such as spruces, pines, firs, and larches, as well as deciduous trees such as aspen, birch, and willow. While mosses and lichens are commonly found in the understory of boreal forests, they do not typically contribute to the overall floral diversity of these ecosystems.
Which answer is correct?
Tree species

Decomposition rates are fairly slow in the Temperate Evergreen Rainforests of the Pacific Northwest.  Select the two main reasons that explain these slow rates.
Group of answer choices

Temperatures are cool when moisture is available for decomposition

Litter quality is poor because these forests are dominated by gymnosperms, which have higher lignin content in their tissues.

Litter quality is poor because these forests are dominated by angiosperms, which have higher lignin content in their tissues.

The parent material of the soils in this region has a high PH and slows microbial activity

These areas are dominated by evergreen trees that rarely senesce their leaves, and so there is simply not enough organic material for fast rates of decomposition.

The two main reasons that explain the slow decomposition rates in the Temperate Evergreen Rainforests of the Pacific Northwest are:
- Litter quality is poor because these forests are dominated by gymnosperms, which have higher lignin content in their tissues.
- Temperatures are cool when moisture is available for decomposition.

The image below shows the devastating impact that fast wind speeds can have on forest canopies.  Considering that the trees in the Temperate Evergreen Rainforest are some of the tallest in the world, why don't these forests experience much wind damage?   
Wind storms are rare in the western US, which is why trees in this region have been able to grow so tall.

Due to the high lignin concentration of gymnosperm wood, the stems of these trees are stronger than the wood of angiosperms.

The dense canopies prevent fast wind speeds from getting down into the canopies and applying force to the trees.

The canonical shape of the canopies, resulting from apical bud dominance, allow wind to pass through the canopies without significant damage.

Because the wood is composed of both vessels and fibers, the wood is both flexible and strong, which is better at resisting wind damage.
The answer is: Due to the high lignin concentration of gymnosperm wood, the stems of these trees are stronger than the wood of angiosperms.

When discussing the forests in the Pacific Northwest I discussed 4 things for which these forests are known. Select the 4 factors from the list below.
Group of answer choices

Giant trees

Heterogeneous canopies

Moss and lichen diversity

Old-growth forests

Juniper expansion

Ozone pollution

A permafrost layer in the soil

The four factors concerning the forests in the Pacific Northwest that were discussed are:
- Giant trees
- Old-growth forests
- Heterogeneous canopies
- Moss and lichen diversity

The forests in the Sierra Nevada Mountain range are known for having a high diversity of Pine species.  What percentage of the pine species in the United States occur in this region?
Group of answer choices

53%

95%

42%

35%
Approximately 50% of the pine species in the United States occur in the Sierra Nevada mountain range.

From the list below, select the answer that best describes the difference between a shrubland and a scrubland.

Group of answer choices

The canopies of individual shrubs are generally overlapping in a shrubland, but are more spread out in a scrubland.

Scrublands are dominated by smaller shrubs with thorns or spines. Shrublands are dominated by shrubs that tend to be taller but don't have thorns or spines.

Scrublands dominate dry areas with saline soils and shrubland dominate dry areas with non-saline soils.

The term 'scrubland' is used in the southwest, but the same type of ecosystem is referred to as a 'shrubland' in other regions.
The answer is: Scrublands are dominated by smaller shrubs with thorns or spines. Shrublands are dominated by shrubs that tend to be taller but don't have thorns or spines.

There are 6 factors that impact the type of vegetation found at a particular site.  Those factors are: climate, disturbance, time, soils, relief, and what else?  Select the answer that completes the list of 6 factors.
Group of answer choices

Organisms

Topography

Precipitation

Fire frequency

Glacial history

Parent material
The sixth factor that impacts the type of vegetation found at a particular site is "parent material."
